The previous style guide
This site replaces the older NCDPI Data Visualization Style Guide that lived as a Tableau workbook. The content here is being refreshed and expanded as we migrate it.

title: Resources

Help

Branding questions: branding@dpi.nc.gov
Design help: graphics@dpi.nc.gov
This guide is published from GitHub.

title: Typography
text:
NCDPI charts, dashboards, and official web materials use Arial (fallback Helvetica) — the agency’s WCAG-compliant workhorse font. This guide deliberately sets its own body text in Arial too, so the words on the page match the words in the charts.

The NC Digital Commons web framework specifies Source Sans Pro for sites that live on nc.gov. This guide is an NCDPI-owned site about the chart palette, so it follows the NCDPI agency standard (Arial) for visual consistency with the charts.

title: Typography
text:
NCDPI charts, dashboards, and official web materials use Arial (fallback Helvetica) — the agency’s WCAG-compliant workhorse font. This guide deliberately sets its own body text in Arial too, so the words on the page match the words in the charts.

The NC Digital Commons web framework specifies Source Sans Pro for sites that live on nc.gov. This guide is an NCDPI-owned site about the chart palette, so it follows the NCDPI agency standard (Arial) for visual consistency with the charts.

title: Typography
text:
Text sizes in charts
These come straight from the brand color-and-type CSV that drives the whole guide:



Role
Size
Color




Chart title
14 pt, bold
 Black #000000


Subtitle / note
11 pt
 Dark grey #525A60


Axis label
11 pt
 Black #000000


Axis value
11 pt
 Dark grey #525A60


Annotation
11 pt
 Plum #842175


Dashboard title
26 pt

title: Typography
text:
Rules

Body and paragraph text is black or DPI Blue.
Don’t color-code text for emphasis — use bold instead.
Section titles and headings on this site are DPI Blue. (In charts, the title is black and the dashboard title is DPI Blue — see the table above.)
If everything stands out, nothing stands out.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Use a sequential palette when the data has a natural order from low to high. NCDPI provides three, because in education “high” isn’t always “good”:
Darker always means more — the dark-is-more convention people read intuitively.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Explore the palettes
Pick a direction and the number of steps:


The steps are equally spaced in color space. Each step is the same perceptual distance from the next, from the neutral end to the full color. So the values they stand for should be equally spaced too — even color steps imply even bins (0–20, 20–40, …), not arbitrary cutoffs.


Don’t let color do all the work. Adjacent steps are close in lightness and won’t all meet WCAG contrast against each other. Keep a labeled legend or value labels, and add a thin white border where segments touch (pie, treemap, stacked, map).


Continuous variants. Each sequence also has a smooth, stepless continuous ramp. Reach for it only where the data is truly continuous and position can’t carry the magnitude — almost always a choropleth map or a heatmap. For anything with countable categories, use the stepped palettes above.

title: Color — sequences
text:
Example
A sequential palette earns its keep where position can’t show magnitude — here a heatmap, where color carries the value. Match the palette to the meaning: a proficiency view uses Sequence to Good; shade a chronic-absenteeism view with Sequence to Bad.

title: Color — highlights
text: The single most useful move in the whole palette: plot everything in neutral grey and use color for the one thing your message is about. This is how you handle “too many categories” without breaking the five-color rule.

title: Color — highlights
text:
Example
Eight regions, all neutral grey except one in navy. The eye lands on the focal region immediately — and you’ve spent only one color to do it.

title: Color — diverging
text:
Use a diverging palette when the scale has a meaningful midpoint and runs in two directions from it — change versus last year, performance versus a target, or Likert agreement. Rust on one side, teal on the other, neutral grey in the middle.
NCDPI provides 3-, 5-, 7-, and 9-step versions so you can match the number of classes in your data. The steps read like a number line — negative through zero to positive (Step -2 … Step 0 … Step +2).

title: Color — diverging
text:
Explore the steps


Steps are symmetric and equally spaced around the midpoint. Match the data to that shape: a 5-step palette fits five ordered classes centered on a true middle (Strongly disagree … Neutral … Strongly agree), not lopsided cutoffs. If your data isn’t centered on a real zero/neutral, a sequence is the better fit.


Don’t let color do all the work. Steps near the midpoint are close in lightness and won’t all meet WCAG contrast against each other. Keep a labeled legend or value labels, and add a thin white border where segments touch.


The School Performance Grade colors (A → F) are the 5-step diverging palette in action: A is teal, C is neutral grey, F is rust. You’ll see it on the pie and map examples.

title: Color — diverging
text:
Example
Likert data is the textbook diverging case: a diverging stacked bar runs from disagree (rust) through neutral (grey) to agree (teal), centered on the neutral response.

title: Maps
text: Maps answer “where?” They’re powerful and familiar, but color-on-geography is one of the least precise encodings — always pair a map with a chart that allows precise comparison.

title: Maps
text:
Choropleth map
Shades each district by a rate (here, the share of students chronically absent). Good for “where is the rate highest?” — bad at communicating exact values, because position is fixed by geography and color is a low-precision encoding. Big rural districts also pull the eye more than small urban ones with the same value.




Choropleth vs. dot-density: which to use. A choropleth answers “where is the rate highest?” — values are normalised to area (percent, per-capita, average), so a small dense district and a large rural one are comparable. A dot-density map answers “where are the things?” — it shows counts in place, so density patterns are honest but rates aren’t. Pick the one that matches your question, or show both.

title: Maps
text:
Dot-density map
Plots each school by location. Honest about where the schools are, but doesn’t support reading any per-district rate from the map alone.




On any map, put a sorted bar or dot plot alongside it. The map shows the pattern; the bar lets people read the values.

title: KPIs & big numbers
text: Sometimes the most effective chart is barely a chart at all — a single number, set large, that answers the one question a reader came with. On a dashboard it is the anchor: the figure the eye lands on first, before it works through the supporting detail. The job of this family is headline, not exploration.

title: KPIs & big numbers
text:
The big number (BAN)
A BAN — Big Aggregate Number (or, less politely, Big A** Number) — is a headline metric shown large, with just enough context to be read at a glance: a comparison and a small trend. It is the dashboard equivalent of a topline sentence.



The number does the work; everything else is there to keep it honest. A bare “55%” tells you nothing about whether that is good, improving, or alarming — so the tile adds a label (what is being measured, and when), a change cue (▲ 0.8 pts vs 2024), and a sparkline (the path that got us here). That is the whole idea: context is what turns a number into a decision.

title: KPIs & big numbers
text:
Anatomy of a big number
A well-built BAN has five parts, and no more:

The label — what this measures and over what period. Small, grey, above the number. (“Grade-level proficiency · 2025.”)
The number — the indicator itself, by far the largest element. This is the only thing a reader is guaranteed to take away.
The comparison — the figure on its own is just a value; pair it with a previous period, a target, or a goal so the reader knows whether to be pleased.
The change cue — the one colored element. Teal for a rise, rust for a fall. Color earns its place here because direction is the judgment.
The supporting micro-chart — a sparkline or tiny bar that shows the trend without axes or labels. It answers “is this a blip or a pattern?”


Color only the change cue. In the example above, the label is grey, the number is near-black, and only the ▲ change is colored. One accent keeps the eye on the single thing that carries a verdict. A BAN wearing four colors is a BAN with no focus.

title: KPIs & big numbers
section: Ten principles for a big number that works
text:
Ten principles for a big number that works
NCDPI’s BAN guidance adapts Aurélien Vautier’s “Big Numbers” framework — the single best treatment of this pattern we’ve found. The four words he opens with are the test every tile should pass: a big number should be actionable, accessible, attainable, and accurate. His ten principles, in our words:

Positioning — place big numbers prominently: the top, or the left, where the reading eye starts (see our dashboard layout guidance on the Z-pattern).
Key insight — a BAN should carry a number worth deciding on, not just a number that was easy to compute. If no one acts on it, it doesn’t earn the size.
Minimalist — clean, simple, free of clutter. The number is the design.
Supporting visuals — pair the number with a small chart (a sparkline, a bar) to show trend or change over time.
Perspective — give the number a frame of reference: a prior period, a target, a goal. This is the difference between “55%” and “55%, up from 45% in 2021.”
Exploration — on an interactive dashboard, a BAN can be an entry point — click it to drill into the detail behind it, or let it reflect the current filter.
Color with care — use color sparingly, to flag a change or an alert, never as decoration (NCDPI: this is the same rule as our color conventions — color means something).
Limit the quantity — a wall of big numbers is no big number at all. Keep a dashboard to a few, so each can still command attention.
Formatting — standardize fonts, decimals, and units across every tile so the set reads as one system. (Round to whole points unless a fraction is the message.)
Actionable — let the number lead somewhere: a drill-down, a linked view, the data behind it. The big number is the start of the question, not the end.

title: KPIs & big numbers
text:
Four ways to add context
The same metric can be dressed for different jobs. Vautier names four common variations — each adds a different kind of context to the headline figure:

The Versus — two numbers side by side (this year vs. last), so the reader makes the comparison directly.
The Storyteller — the number plus a short sentence of narrative (“up 0.8 points; on pace to clear 56% next year”). Best when the so-what needs spelling out.
The Textual Versus — the comparison folded into a compact line of text rather than two large figures. Quietest of the four; good when space is tight.
The Bullet graph — the number measured against a target in a small bar. When the question is “are we hitting the goal?”, this is the strongest of the four — and it’s a chart type in its own right (below).

title: KPIs & big numbers
text:
The bullet chart
A bullet chart is the natural next step from a BAN: it keeps the compact, glance-able footprint but adds an explicit target. The bar is the actual value; the tick is the goal; the faint background band gives a qualitative range. It answers “did we hit it?” for a whole list of categories at once.




The bullet chart also appears under difference from a baseline — actual-vs-target is a deviation story as much as a KPI one. Same chart, two jobs.

title: KPIs & big numbers
text:
NCDPI specifics

Label school years by the spring. Write 2025, not “2024–25” — the BAN has no room to explain a hyphenated range, and the spring year is our house convention.
Valence lives on the change cue, not the number. The proficiency figure itself is near-black; teal/rust is reserved for the direction of change, where a judgment is actually being made.


The ten principles and the four variations on this page adapt Aurélien Vautier’s “10 Commandments of Big Numbers” framework — see his work at datavizclarity.com. To see the four variations live and interactive, Felipe Sebben rebuilt them faithfully in Tableau: The 10 Commandments of Big Numbers in Practice. The NCDPI examples above use real, public accountability data and the NCDPI brand palette.

title: Distributions
text: Reach for a distribution chart when the spread is the story — not a single average, but how values are arranged around it.

title: Distributions
text:
Histogram
Shows the shape of one numeric variable: where schools cluster, and whether the spread is symmetric, skewed, or has two humps.

title: Distributions
text:
Box plot
Compares several distributions at once — median, quartiles, and outliers side by side. For staff and analyst audiences; add a one-line explainer for the public.




A single average can hide a bimodal or skewed distribution. If two schools both “average a C” but one is all-C and the other is half-A/half-F, the distribution is the honest picture.

title: Relationships
text: When you want to show whether two numbers move together, plot them against each other. Position on two scales is the most accurate read there is.

title: Relationships
text:
Scatterplot
Each dot is a school. The shape reveals direction, strength, and outliers. Add a trend line if the relationship is the headline.

title: Relationships
text:
Bubble chart
Add a third measure as bubble area — but read area as approximate, never precise, and keep the count modest.




Correlation isn’t causation, and a trend line invites that leap. Annotate what the relationship does and doesn’t mean for your audience.

title: Tooltips
text: A tooltip is the little box that appears when a reader hovers over a mark. Used well, it lets a reader check their reading of a chart. Used carelessly — which is the default in most tools — it dumps raw field names and unformatted numbers, and adds noise instead of clarity.

title: Tooltips
section: The one rule: a tooltip supplements, it never carries the message
text:
The one rule: a tooltip supplements, it never carries the message
The science is clear that the title and text are what readers remember — when people recall a visualization, the title is the single most-mentioned element. The main point therefore has to live where everyone can see it: the message title, the subtitle, the direct labels. A tooltip is invisible until hovered, so anything you hide inside one is, for most readers, not there at all.
Research on interactive charts reinforces this from a second direction: interacting with a chart (hovering, filtering, drilling) can actually lower how much specific information a reader retains, because the effort of working the interface competes with encoding the data. Interaction earns its keep through engagement — the sense of exploring for oneself — not through recall. So lean on tooltips for exploration and confirmation, never as the only home for a fact.
The practical consequence:

Never put anything essential only in a tooltip. Keyboard users, touch users, screen-reader users, and anyone reading a printed or PowerPoint export never see it. The data table and alt text are what carry the chart for them.
Don’t repeat what’s already directly labeled. If the value is on the bar, a tooltip showing the same value is pure noise.

title: Tooltips
text:
Two tooltips worth building
For NCDPI work, almost every useful tooltip is one of two kinds.

1. The sentence tooltip — help the reader check their understanding
On a chart with a clear message, the most useful tooltip puts into a sentence what the reader is looking at, by synthesizing the subtitle’s contract (metric, unit, geography, period) with the value of the mark under the cursor. It lets a reader confirm they’ve read the chart correctly.
On the proficiency-levels column chart, hovering the rust “Level 1 or 2” bar would read:

45% of students scored at Level 1 or 2 (not proficient) on tests across all subjects and grades in 2025.

That single sentence stitches together the what (Level 1 or 2 = not proficient), the who/where/when (all subjects and grades, 2025), and the value (45%) — exactly the contract the subtitle already promises, now spoken for one specific mark. Best on charts a general audience reads slowly, where a plain-language gloss removes any doubt.


2. The value tooltip — zoom in on a crowded chart
When a chart has too many marks to label — a scatter of all 2,500-odd schools, a time series with a value at every year — direct labels would collide, so the marks go unlabeled. Here the tooltip does one small job: reveal the value of the mark you’re pointing at. Hovering a point simply reads:

85%

No sentence, no fuss — the chart already established the context; the reader just wants the number behind this one dot. This is the honest use of interactivity to “manage visual noise”: the overview stays clean, and the precise value is one hover away for anyone who wants it.

title: Tooltips
section: Formatting a tooltip so it doesn’t undo your chart
text:
Formatting a tooltip so it doesn’t undo your chart
If you do build one — in Tableau, Power BI, or a hand-authored Vega-Lite spec — hold it to the same standard as the rest of the chart:

Whole words, not field names. “Grade-level proficiency,” never prof_glp_pct.
Match the chart’s number formatting. Same rounding, same units (%, pts), same spring-year labeling (2025, not 2024-25). A tooltip that shows 0.4503 under a chart that says 45% reads as a different number.
One mark, one tooltip — keep it short. Don’t list every field in the data behind the point. Pick the value and the minimum context that makes it legible.
Decide if it earns its place at all. A fully labeled, well-titled chart often needs no tooltip. Add one when it genuinely helps a reader explore or confirm — not by reflex.


A good tooltip is a check, not a crutch. If a reader needs the hover to grasp the main point, the chart hasn’t done its job — fix the title, subtitle, or labels first, and let the tooltip stay an optional second look.

title: Dashboard elements
text: A dashboard is a set of charts that share context. The chrome around them should be calm and predictable so the data stands out.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI dashboard



Anatomy of an NCDPI dashboard — title and subtitle, BAN row, supporting charts, filter rail, and footer chrome.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Titles and text

Dashboard title: DPI Blue, 26 pt. Subtitle: dark grey, 16 pt.
Help text (how to read a chart, how to use a filter): Plum #842175, 11 pt — the same treatment as chart annotations. Help text and annotations are both meta-overlays on data, so they share a color so users learn the convention once.
Body text is black or DPI Blue — never color-coded for emphasis.


Common dashboard chrome
Every NCDPI dashboard should carry, somewhere visible (usually the footer):

Data last updated — a date, plus the refresh cadence if known (“Updated 2025-09-15, monthly”).
Contact — a name or shared inbox for questions and corrections.
NCDPI logo — small, top-left or footer, so attribution is unambiguous.
Glossary or help link — for any term a non-expert reader might stumble on.

These four items add almost no visual weight but answer the questions every new viewer has within thirty seconds.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Controls and filters

Group filters together in a consistent place (top or right rail) so users learn where to look once.
Label the selected state clearly (darker) versus unselected (grey).
Default to the most useful view, not an empty one — don’t make users configure the dashboard before it says anything.


Keep the filter count low
Every filter is one more decision the user has to make before the dashboard answers anything (Hick’s Law — choice time grows with the number of options). Aim for the minimum set of filters that lets users answer the question the dashboard is built for. If you can’t answer “what does this filter unlock?” in one sentence, the filter probably shouldn’t be there.


When you can’t trim, use progressive disclosure
Sometimes a stakeholder mandates more filters than really fit. In that case, don’t put them all on the page at once — hide secondary filters behind a collapsible panel (Tableau: dynamic zone visibility) or a “More filters” toggle. Show the two or three most-used filters by default and let the user reveal the rest if they need them.


Prefer cross-filtering over filter controls
The most intuitive filter is the chart itself. Let the user click a bar on a top-left chart and watch the rest of the dashboard re-scope to that selection. Cross-filtering replaces chrome with direct manipulation — fewer dropdowns, more discovery.

title: Dashboard elements
text:
Layout

Reading order: Z-pattern, top-left first
Western readers scan dashboards in a rough Z: top-left first, eye sweeps across to the top-right, drops down and across again to the bottom-right. The top-left position earns the most attention — put your most important number or chart there.


Drill down as you move down and right
Pair the Z-pattern with a progressive zoom. Top-left should be the 30,000-ft view — the headline number or trend. As the eye moves down and to the right, content gets progressively more granular: state → region → district → school. This matches Shneiderman’s mantra for information visualization: overview first, zoom and filter, details on demand.


Big Aggregate Numbers (BANs) at the top
The headline number for the whole dashboard usually deserves to be the very first thing the eye lands on. A row of BANs — single, large numerals with a short label — anchors the dashboard, sets the scale, and gives the rest of the charts a reference point. Use them sparingly (three to five at most) and label the time period clearly.

BAN page coming soon. BANs are not yet listed as a chart type on this site — a dedicated page is on the roadmap. In the meantime, keep them simple: one big number, one short label, one tiny period of comparison (“↑ 2.1 pts vs. 2022”).



Interactivity earns its keep
Hover for detail, click to highlight, filter to focus. In a dashboard you can show more than in a static chart because the user controls what to look at — but every interactive feature is one more thing to discover. Make the common path obvious.

Dashboards are where highlight-one-on-grey shines: plot every series in grey and let the user click to spotlight the one they care about — no color overload, any number of categories.

title: Chart Gallery
text: Every worked example from the Chart Chooser on one page, grouped by purpose. Use it to browse what an on-brand NCDPI version of each chart type looks like — or to find a type by eye when you’d rather scan than answer the chooser’s questions.

title: Chart Gallery
text:
Used elsewhere in the guide
Two more specs that aren’t chooser examples but appear on the color pages — included here so this page shows every live chart in the guide.

  Category-group bar
  charts/groups__category-bar.vl.json
  


  Spotlight (highlight) bar
  charts/highlight__spotlight-bar.vl.json

title: Small multiples
text: A small-multiples chart repeats the same little chart once per category, on a shared scale, in a grid. It is the most reliable cure for “I have too many categories” — and it’s badly under-used at NCDPI, so reach for it more.

title: Small multiples
text:
Why they work

No color overload. Each panel uses one color, so the five-color ceiling never binds, no matter how many categories you have.
Honest comparison. A shared scale means a tall bar in one panel really is taller than a short bar in another.
Pattern at a glance. The eye sweeps the grid and spots the outlier panel immediately.

title: Small multiples
text:
How to build them well

Keep every panel on the same scale — that’s the whole point.
Order the panels meaningfully (by value, region, time) rather than alphabetically.
One color across all panels; highlight a reference (the state average) the same way in each.
Stop around 20–30 panels — past that, each is too small to read.

title: Best practices
text: A handful of principles, drawn from our data visualization literature review, explain why the rules on this site are the rules. Read these once and most chart decisions answer themselves.

title: Best practices
section: NCDPI standing rules, in one place
text:
NCDPI standing rules, in one place


Five colors maximum (three or four is better).
Promote small multiples — they’re under-used here.
Stacked bars: 2–3 segments only. Beyond that, separate bars on a common baseline.
Break lines at missing years (e.g., the 2020–21 COVID gap) — never interpolate.
Graph the delta — when the message is a difference, plot the difference itself.

title: Axis scales
text:
How far you let an axis run is a design decision, not a default. For a bounded, aspirational metric — graduation rate, proficiency, attendance, any percent where 100% is the real goal — start the value axis at the goal by default. The reader should see how far there is left to go, not just how the categories rank against each other.
But there’s a cost: when every value sits in a narrow band near the top, a full 0–100 axis makes them look identical. The same eight regions below differ by 5.5 points — invisible on the goal-anchored axis, obvious when zoomed.

title: Axis scales
text:
The rule

Default to the real ceiling. Anchor a bounded aspirational metric to 0–100 (or 0–whatever the goal is) unless you have a reason not to. It keeps the distance to the goal honest.
Bars are non-negotiable: always zero-based. A bar encodes its value as length, so a cut bar lies about magnitude. If you want to zoom, you must switch to a position mark — a dot or a line — where the value is read from position, not bar length. (That’s why the example above uses dots.)
Zoom only with cause, and signal it. When the message is the difference between near-tied values, a zoomed axis is fair for dots and lines — but make the cut obvious (label the axis range, don’t start it at a round-looking number that reads as zero).

title: Axis scales
text:
Having it both ways
When you genuinely need the goal context and the fine variation, you have three honest moves, in order of preference:

Goal-anchored axis — when “how far from the goal” is the message, just use 0–100 and stop.
Honest zoom with a goal reference — zoom the axis to the data, but keep the goal in view: draw a faint reference line or band at the target and annotate the gap. One chart, both facts.
Paired combo — show the goal-anchored chart and the zoomed chart together (as above), labeled, so the reader sees the trade-off directly. This is the most explicit teaching version.


A true picture-in-picture inset — a small zoomed chart floating inside the full-range one — is not possible in our Vega-Lite charts (Vega-Lite lays views out side by side, not overlapping). If you specifically want that magnified-inset look, build it in matplotlib (an inset axes) and place it as an image, or author it in full Vega. For the live, interactive charts on this site, the paired combo above is the equivalent.

title: Accessibility
text: Accessibility is not a box we check at the end. It is the whole point of a chart: everyone who comes to a visualization should be able to leave with its takeaway. This page lays out how we think about that at NCDPI — which is, in a few places, deliberately more nuanced than a strict checklist reading of the rules. We explain why, and we show our work against the actual standard.

title: Accessibility
text:
Two levels of accessibility
It helps to separate two things that both get called “accessibility.”

The over-riding level — can people get the story? A chart is inaccessible if a reader can’t extract its meaning — and most often that barrier has nothing to do with disability. It’s jargon, an unexplained metric, a missing unit, a buried takeaway, or an assumption that the reader is a “math person.” Clearing that barrier — for the non-specialist, the busy stakeholder, the person new to the subject — is the first job, and it serves everyone.
The technical level — ADA, Section 508, WCAG. Nested inside the first is the layer most people mean by “accessibility”: contrast ratios, non-color cues, alt text, keyboard and screen-reader support. This layer is real, it’s now legally required (see below), and we take it seriously. But it is a means to the over-riding goal, not the goal itself.

Keeping these straight matters, because the techniques that serve one often serve the other — a clear message title helps both a screen-reader user and a hurried director — and because the technical layer is best understood as one of several ways we get a reader to the takeaway, not the only way.

title: Accessibility
text:
Equivalent access, not identical access
Here is the principle that drives the rest of this page:

Our goal is that every reader can reach the takeaway — but how they reach it need not be the same for every reader.

If a chart offers the story through several independent channels — direct labels so the numbers are readable without decoding color, an auxiliary data table, and good alt text — then no single visual element has to carry the entire accessibility burden on its own. That is not a loophole. As the next section shows, it is exactly how the standard is written.

title: Accessibility
section: What WCAG actually says — and what it doesn’t
text:
What WCAG actually says — and what it doesn’t
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1, Level AA, are the technical standard. It’s worth quoting the relevant criteria precisely, because the text draws its lines in specific places — and not always where a strict reading assumes.

Use of Color (1.4.1, Level A)

“Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.”

What it requires: a redundant, non-color cue wherever color carries meaning. What it does not require: removing color, nor that the colors on any palette (categorical, sequential, diverging) mandate a particular contrast ratio between one another. (A 3:1 difference in lightness is one way to supply the “additional visual means” the criterion asks for — but it is only one way; direct labels, white separators, and an auxiliary table satisfy it equally.) So when we add direct labels, white separators between abutting fills, and an auxiliary table, color is no longer “the only means” — and 1.4.1 is satisfied. (W3C — Understanding 1.4.1)


Non-text Contrast (1.4.11, Level AA)

“The visual presentation of [graphical objects] have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent color(s): Parts of graphics required to understand the content, except when a particular presentation of graphics is essential to the information being conveyed.”

The scope is the operative phrase: “required to understand the content.” The W3C’s own guidance lists when a graphic doesn’t need to meet 3:1 — including when “the information is available in another form.”

This is the crux. A faint gridline behind directly-labeled values, or a neutral #B7B9BB context bar whose magnitude is encoded by length and a direct label and an auxiliary table, is not “required to understand the content” — the information is available in another form. Such elements fall outside 1.4.11’s scope; they are not violations of it.
So “if it’s visible, it must meet 3:1” is stricter than the standard actually requires. We hold the contrast line — we just hold it on the elements that genuinely carry the meaning. (W3C — Understanding 1.4.11)

A stricter reading exists — and we’ve weighed it. Some excellent dataviz-accessibility resources — notably Chartability (in our resources below) — apply a blanket 3:1 to all “geometries,” deliberately going beyond WCAG 1.4.11’s “required to understand” scope. That is a defensible, conservative ceiling, and we treat it as one. But where such a rule diverges from the standard, we follow WCAG’s actual scoping: an element that is not required to understand the content, because the information is genuinely available another way, is not a violation. We name the difference on purpose — holding the WCAG line where it’s drawn is a considered choice, not an oversight.
One limit on this exception: it holds only where direct labeling is actually feasible. When data density makes labels impossible — a 115-district choropleth, a school-level scatter of thousands of points — the marks themselves become required to understand the content, and the label exception no longer applies. Then the auxiliary data table (below) becomes the primary equivalent pathway. Better still, treat the density as the real problem: aggregate, or break the data into small multiples, so the chart stays both legible and labelable. (Note this cuts the other way for sequential fills: adjacent steps of a color ramp still need not contrast 3:1 with each other. W3C’s own guidance treats a measurement gradient under the essential exception — forcing 3:1 between neighboring shades would undermine the ramp itself — and the reader takes the value from the legend and labels, not from telling two shades apart.)


Contrast — Minimum (1.4.3, Level AA) — the line we do not bend

Text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 (or 3:1 for large text — 18pt, or 14pt bold).

Note the boundary: 1.4.3 governs text, and it has no “required to understand” exception. So the nuance above applies only to non-text graphical objects — gridlines, fills, strokes. Text always meets contrast: data labels, axis values, titles, subtitles, and annotations are held to 4.5:1, full stop. This is what keeps our position honest — we are precise about where a contrast requirement applies, not casual about whether it does. (W3C — Understanding 1.4.3)


When a chart isn’t the right vehicle: conforming alternatives
A multi-chart dashboard read aloud by a screen reader, even with a good tab order, is often a poor experience — the structure that makes a dashboard powerful visually does not serialize well into speech. WCAG anticipates this with the conforming alternative version: an accessible equivalent (here, a well-structured data table plus a plain-language summary of the takeaway) that “provides all of the same information,” is kept as up to date, and is reachable from the original.
Two honest caveats: WCAG treats this as a fallback — the preferred path is to make content directly accessible — and the alternative only counts if we actually provide it and link to it. A conforming alternative is a safety net, not a license to write the dashboard itself off: keyboard navigation, sensible tab order, and clear focus states still matter, because they serve low-vision, keyboard-only, and motor-impaired readers who do use the visual. (W3C — Understanding Conformance)
And whatever the dashboard does interactively, don’t trap the keyboard: a keyboard user must be able to tab into and back out of every interactive widget — a filter, a legend toggle, an embedded viz — with standard Tab / Shift-Tab, never getting stuck inside one. This is its own Level A requirement (WCAG 2.1.2, No Keyboard Trap) and a frequent late-stage audit failure in tools like Tableau.


The legal footing
In April 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice finalized a rule under ADA Title II adopting WCAG 2.1 Level AA for state and local government websites and apps. A subsequent interim rule (April 2026) extended the deadlines by a year — moving the dates only; the WCAG 2.1 Level AA standard itself did not change. As a state agency, NCDPI is on the April 26, 2027 compliance date. That deadline is real, and it’s why the field is leaning toward strict, bright-line readings right now. Our framing meets the standard — we get there through redundant pathways, including conforming alternatives — rather than treating any element as exempt from the goal of access. (ADA.gov — the web rule)

title: Accessibility
text:
Techniques
How the principle becomes practice. None of these is sufficient alone; together they are what “equivalent access” means.

On the chart
Color and contrast. Use our palettes (they’re built for this) and make text meet 4.5:1. Don’t chase the impossible standard of every categorical hue contrasting 3:1 with every other and with white at once — at NCDPI we found that nearly unachievable, and the colors you’d be forced into carry their own unwanted connotations. The right move isn’t darker colors; it’s not making color carry the meaning alone. See Color rules and the highlight palette.
Never color alone. Whatever color says, something else should say too — position, a direct label, a shape, an annotation. This is 1.4.1, and it’s also just good charting.
Label directly. A number printed next to its mark is readable by everyone, removes the decode-the-legend step, and is the single most powerful accessibility move available. It’s also what lets a faint gridline or neutral fill stay faint (see the keystone above).
Annotate when it helps. A short note that states the takeaway in words — in our annotation Plum — gives every reader the point directly, not just those who can decode the encoding.

A note on pattern fills. Hatching and polka-dot fills are a legitimate redundancy channel — they help colorblind readers, and they survive black-and-white printing where color collapses entirely. But they add visual noise, and they can pull the eye toward the texture inside a bar rather than the bar’s edge, which is where length encodes the value. So treat them as a hierarchy: reach first for direct labels and white separators; reserve patterns for artifacts that may be reproduced without color.



Supplemental to the chart
Alt text. Every chart carries a text description (in our specs, the description field, which becomes the chart’s accessible label). Good alt text states the takeaway, not just “a bar chart” — it’s the screen-reader user’s version of the message title. A template that keeps it consistent:

[Chart type] showing [data scope], where [the primary trend or takeaway]. Full data in the accompanying table.

For example: “Line chart of North Carolina’s four-year cohort graduation rate, 2019–2025, showing a steady climb to a record high in 2025. Full data in the table below.”
Supporting tables. Offer the underlying numbers as a real, well-structured table (see Table elements). This is both a 1.4.11 “available in another form” pathway and the backbone of a conforming alternative for complex dashboards.
Natural-language query. Increasingly, readers can ask a dashboard a question in plain words and get an answer back. Where the tooling supports it, this is another door into the same data — especially valuable for readers who find the visual itself a barrier. Treat it as a supplement, not a substitute, for the techniques above. See also Tooltips for putting the value into words on hover.

title: Accessibility
text:
Take this page with you
This page as a single Markdown file — for offline reference, or to feed to an AI assistant. Generated from the same source as the live page, so it stays in sync.
⬇ Download (Markdown)

title: Accessibility
text:
Resources
A short, curated set — covering both the technical layer and the story-for-everyone layer:

W3C WAI — Complex Images tutorial — the authoritative source on describing charts and graphics for WCAG.
Chartability (Frank Elavsky) — 50 testable heuristics for evaluating data-visualization accessibility; tool-agnostic and used across government agencies. The best dataviz-specific resource going.
Do No Harm Guide: Applying Equity Awareness in Data Visualization (Schwabish & Feng, Urban Institute) — the story-for-everyone layer: framing, language, and ordering that make data equitable and inclusive.
Datawrapper — Accessibility and designing for colorblindness (Lisa Charlotte Muth) — the clearest practical writing on the craft.
An intro to designing accessible data visualizations (Sarah L. Fossheim) — a clean, example-driven primer.
The ADA Title II web rule (ADA.gov) — the legal requirement and what it means for public agencies.

title: Annotation
text: A chart that makes the reader figure out the point often loses them. Do the interpreting for them.

title: Annotation
section: Write the takeaway into the title
text:
Write the takeaway into the title
A title like “Average SPG score by region” describes the chart. A title like “Five regions gained ground since 2022; the Sandhills lagged” tells the reader what to see. Lead with the message; let the axis labels handle the description.

title: Annotation
text:
Label directly, annotate the story

Direct-label lines and bars instead of relying on a legend (up to ~7 items).
Call out the one or two points that carry the argument — the outlier, the crossover, the target line — with a short text note in plum #842175, the brand annotation color.
Mark context the data needs: a policy change, a target, the COVID gap.

title: Annotation
text:
Less chart, more meaning
Annotation is not decoration. Each note should answer a question the reader would otherwise have to ask. If a viewer can look at the chart and immediately say what it means, the annotation has done its job.

This pairs with the perceptual hierarchy: pick the chart that encodes the data accurately, then annotate it so the meaning is as easy to read as the values.

title: Color rules
text: Color is the most abused encoding in agency charts. Three rules keep it honest.

title: Color rules
text:
1. Five colors is the ceiling
People can hold only a handful of categorical hues apart before search slows and errors climb; three to four is the comfortable range. When you have more categories, the answer is never more colors — it’s highlight-one-on-grey or small multiples.

title: Color rules
text:
2. Dark is more
On a sequential scale, people read darker as a larger value by default. Honour it: don’t make your lightest shade the biggest number. NCDPI’s sequential palettes are built dark-is-more already.

title: Color rules
text:
3. Color should mean something

Match the palette to the data: categorical for unordered groups, sequential for ordered, diverging for a meaningful midpoint.
Make “good” and “bad” directions deliberate — teal for good, rust for bad.
Never rely on color alone. Pair it with labels, position, or shape, so the chart still works for color-blind readers and in greyscale.


Run any color-on-color text combination through the WebAIM contrast checker. DPI Aqua at full strength is not accessible for small text — use Medium #077890 or Dark #006174 aqua instead.

title: The perceptual hierarchy
text: Some visual encodings are read more accurately than others. Cleveland & McGill’s classic experiments, replicated many times since, rank them roughly like this:

title: The perceptual hierarchy
text:
What it means in practice

To let people read precise values, encode with position or length — a dot plot or bar, not a heatmap.
Use color and area for the broad pattern (a map, a treemap), and pair them with a position/length chart when the exact numbers matter.
This is exactly why the Chart Chooser, when in doubt, prefers the chart higher on this list.


Pie charts sit mid-hierarchy on angle — which is why they’re fine for “which slice is biggest?” with a few slices, and poor for comparing many similar slices. The rule (“five slices, no more”) falls straight out of the science.

title: Chart Chooser
text:
Answer a few quick questions about your data and what you want to show. You’ll get one to three chart types that fit — each with a small, on-brand NCDPI example and tips for building it well. This follows the same evidence-based logic as our chart-selection guidance; it never recommends a chart that misleads.

  The Chart Chooser needs JavaScript. See the Chart types gallery for the same guidance.

title: Chart elements
text: The non-data parts of a chart should recede so the data can speak. NCDPI defaults keep them quiet and consistent.

title: Chart elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI chart
Every piece of the chart below is set by the brand spec — fonts, sizes, colors, and spacing — so a reader who has seen one NCDPI chart already knows how to read the next one.



Anatomy of an NCDPI chart — labeled callouts for title, subtitle, axis labels, axis values, data marks, annotation, gridlines, and source note.

title: Chart elements
text:
Gridlines, axes, and rulers

Gridlines are light grey #DEDEDE — present enough to support reading a value, faint enough to disappear behind the data.
Axis rulers and dividers use the same light grey. Drop the top and right spines entirely.
Reference lines (a target, the state average) are DPI Blue #003A70 so they read as deliberate, not as data.

title: Chart elements
text:
The zero-baseline rule
Bars and areas encode value by length or area, so they must start at zero. Truncating the axis exaggerates differences and is the single most common way a chart misleads. Lines and dot plots may use a non-zero baseline — but say so if you do.

title: Chart elements
text:
Labels over legends
When there are seven or fewer items, label them directly rather than making the reader bounce between a legend and the data. Reserve legends for when direct labels would collide.

Declutter, don’t decorate. Every gridline, tick, and label should earn its place. If removing it doesn’t cost the reader anything, remove it.

title: Table elements
text: Sometimes a table is the right answer — when readers need to look up exact values. Make it scannable.

title: Table elements
text:
Example

North Carolina public schools and student population by urbanicity, 2023–24


Urbanicity
Schools
Student pop.
% of NC student pop.




City
612
472,300
31.4%


Suburb
498
418,900
27.8%


Town
331
186,200
12.4%


Rural
1,074
427,500
28.4%



Total
2,515
1,504,900
100.0%





Source: NCDPI school directory, 2023–24. Counts exclude charters with statewide enrollment.

title: Table elements
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI table
Same idea as the chart anatomy — name each part, then its spec. A table simply has fewer parts. Match these and it reads as a clean look-up grid.



Part
Specification




Title (the <caption>)
14 pt, bold,  Black #000000 — sized to sit above the header row in the hierarchy


Header row
White text on  DPI Blue #003A70 — short, strong labels


Row header
Leftmost cell of each row; left-aligned, regular weight


Row banding
 10% DPI Blue #E6EDF6 on alternate rows — guides the eye across a row without shouting


Dividers
 Light grey #DEDEDE


Total row
2 px top rule, bold, no fill — separates the summary from the data


Source note
10 pt,  Dark grey #525A60 — a table's source note sits one step below the brand's 11 pt chart note, so it recedes

title: Table elements
text:
Alignment

Numbers align right and share decimal places, so magnitudes line up visually.
Text aligns left.
Headers match the alignment of the column beneath them.

title: Table elements
text:
Make tables do more

Sort by the column that matters, not alphabetically by default.
Add an embedded sparkline column to show a trend per row without a separate chart.
Lightly shade or mark the values that carry the message — don’t make readers hunt.

A few small in-cell techniques — one row each. They borrow the chart palette so the table still reads as NCDPI:

In-cell techniques (illustrative)







Technique
Example
Reach for it when…




Sparkline



each row has a short trend (say, attendance 2019–2024) and a full line chart would be overkill. Color the line navy #003A70; dot the last point.


In-cell data bar
86  



readers compare magnitudes down a column at a glance — the bar length does work the bare number can't. Keep the value visible alongside it.


Heat cell
12 47 93
spotting highs and lows across a grid. Shade with Sequence to Neutral (light grey → #5085BC), dark = higher. Mind contrast if you also print the value: dark ink on the darkest steps fails WCAG AA for small text (~4.2:1), and white text is worse — so keep the in-cell number only where the shade is light, or show the value outside the cell.




One enrichment per table, not all three at once. These are accents on a look-up grid — if every column has a bar or a heat tint, you’ve built a cluttered chart, not a table.

title: Table elements
text:
Accessibility

Most NCDPI tables are built in Tableau, PowerPoint, or Word — not hand-coded. The principles here apply in every tool: give the table a real title/caption, use genuine header rows (not bold text faking a header), keep one value per cell (avoid merged cells), and never use a table just for page layout. The HTML attributes shown below (scope, headers/id) are how you satisfy these when you hand-code a table — in Tableau and Office, use the built-in table, crosstab, and caption features and they handle most of it for you.

A data table is one of the few places where structure directly affects whether a screen-reader user can use the table at all — so the underlying markup (or your tool’s accessibility features) matters more here than it does for a chart.

Use <th scope="col"> on column headers (and scope="row" on the leading cell of each row where it makes sense). WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships requires that the table’s structure be programmatically conveyable — scope is how you do that.
Give every table a <caption> or aria-label. The caption answers “what is this table about?” before the screen reader starts reading cells.
Avoid merged header cells. WCAG doesn’t ban them outright, but screen-reader support for merged cells is uneven, and they make linear navigation confusing. If you truly need a merged header (a complex multi-level table), mark it up with explicit headers / id attributes so each data cell points to the headers that govern it.
Don’t use a table for layout. If the goal is page layout, use CSS — not <table>.


A simple, well-marked-up table beats a clever, complex one every time. If you find yourself reaching for merged cells, ask whether two simple tables would tell the same story better.

title: Table elements
section: Pair charts with an accessible table
text:
Pair charts with an accessible table
Even a perfect chart leaves screen-reader users out of the conversation. For any complex chart in a dashboard or slide deck, give them a way to reach the underlying numbers.

In Tableau: add a “View data” button that toggles a hidden cross-tab via dynamic zone visibility. Default-hidden so it doesn’t crowd the visual; one click for users who need it.
In PowerPoint: put the chart on one slide and add a “Data table” hyperlink to a companion slide that holds the corresponding cross-tab.
In a web report: offer a “Show table” toggle beneath the chart, or link to a downloadable CSV.


A table answers “what is the exact value?”; a chart answers “what is the pattern?” If your reader needs both — or if accessibility requires both — give them both.

title: Bar charts
text: The bar chart is the workhorse of comparison: it encodes values as length on a common baseline, which is one of the most accurate things the human eye can do.

title: Bar charts
text:
Comparing categories
When you have one number per category, sort by value and use a single color. With more than five categories you do not need a different color for each bar — position already does the work.




Always start the value axis at zero. Bars encode magnitude by length, so a truncated axis exaggerates differences and misleads the reader.

title: Difference from a baseline
text: When the difference itself is the message — change from last year, above/below a target, or the gap between two groups — plot the difference directly.

title: Difference from a baseline
text:
Graph the delta
Don’t make the reader subtract two bars by eye. A diverging bar against a zero line puts the change front and centre, and a constant gap stays the same size everywhere (two raw lines make a fixed gap look like it shrinks as values rise).

title: Difference from a baseline
section: Dumbbell: the gap between two points
text:
Dumbbell: the gap between two points
A dumbbell keeps both values but draws attention to the distance between them.

title: Difference from a baseline
text:
Rating-scale (Likert) data
Centre a diverging stacked bar on the neutral response: disagree extends left, agree extends right. Never use a pie for survey data.




The survey numbers above are synthetic demo data, not real NCDPI results.

title: Chart types
text:
Charts are organised here by the job they do — the message you want your audience to take away — not by the data type. Not sure which job you have? The Chart Chooser will walk you to a recommendation.
Every example uses real, public NCDPI school data and the NCDPI brand palette.


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
    
    Compare and rank
    Bar charts, dots, lollipops, dumbbells
  

  
    
      
        
        
          
          
          
          
          
          
        
      
    
    Change over time
    Lines, small multiples, slope charts
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
      
    
    Parts of a whole
    Pie, stacked bar, waffle
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Distribution
    Histogram, box plot
  

  
    
      
        
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
          
        
      
    
    Relationships
    Scatter, bubble
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Difference from a baseline
    Diverging bars, dumbbells, Likert
  

  
    
      
        
        
        
        
      
    
    Geographic patterns
    Choropleth, point, hex maps
  

  
    
      
        92%
      
    
    Headline number
    BAN (big number), bullet chart, KPI tile

title: Lines & change over time
text: Use a line when the message is the trend — direction and rate of change across time. The connecting line emphasises continuity, so only use it for genuinely sequential (time) data.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
A few series
Keep it to about five colored lines. Direct-label the ends rather than making the reader bounce to a legend.




Break the line at missing years. NC issued no School Performance Grades in 2020 and 2021. A line drawn straight through that gap would invent two years of data. Show the break.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
Many series → small multiples
More than five lines becomes spaghetti. Give each category its own panel on a shared scale instead. Small multiples are under-used at NCDPI — reach for them.

title: Lines & change over time
text:
Two time points → slope chart
When you only have a “before” and “after,” a slope chart shows each item’s value at both points and the direction of change, and makes rank reversals pop.

title: Parts of a whole
text: Part-to-whole charts answer “what share does each piece make up?” They work best when there are only a few pieces.

title: Parts of a whole
text:
Pie chart
Fine with five slices or fewer, and genuinely good for “which is biggest?” Order slices largest-to-smallest and label them directly.




Five slices is the ceiling. Beyond that, angle judgements collapse — group the small categories into “Other,” or switch to a sorted bar chart.

title: Parts of a whole
text:
Stacked bars: keep them shallow
A 100% stacked bar shows composition across categories, but only the bottom segment sits on a common baseline. Keep stacked bars to 2–3 segments. With four or more, the middle segments float and can’t be compared — use separate bars on a common baseline (grouped bars or small multiples) instead.

title: Color — category groups
text:
Use the group palettes for distinct, unordered categories — regions, subjects, school types. The palettes are pre-built for two through six categories so you never have to guess. Stop at five; if you need more, see Highlights or use small multiples.
These swatches are generated live from the brand color file.
Two categories
Three categories
Four categories
Five categories (the practical maximum)
Six categories (use only when truly unavoidable)

title: Color — category groups
text:
Example
Four locales, four brand colors applied in order — with a white border so neighbours stay crisp. Position and labels do the identifying; color just groups.

title: Color lookup
text: Every color in the NCDPI chart system, searchable. Filter by palette, element, or hex code. This table is generated directly from the brand color file, so it never drifts out of date.

title: Color — overview
text:
The NCDPI chart palette is built for four jobs. Pick the palette that matches the kind of data, not your favorite color.
Throughout this guide we name colors by their HEX value (e.g., #5085BC), not RGB. Every value lives in the searchable Color lookup.

title: Color — overview
section: The four jobs at a glance
text:
The four jobs at a glance
Grey recedes; color carries the message. Each panel below is the same simple bar chart with a different palette doing its one job — highlight one value, separate categories, show an ordered sequence, or flag a good/bad outcome.

title: Color — overview
section: The one rule that matters most
text:
The one rule that matters most

Five distinct colors is the ceiling — three or four is better. Past five hues, people can’t reliably tell categories apart. When you have more, don’t add colors: highlight one against grey, or use small multiples (one small panel per category). Every extra hue is one more thing the reader has to decode — keep the count low so their attention goes to the differences that matter, not to working out which color means which category.

title: Color — overview
text:
Category order
When you do color-code categories, use the brand order so charts stay consistent — Blue → Orange → Purple → Green → Gold → Plum:


See Category groups for how many to use at each step. Never rely on color alone — pair it with labels or position.

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
Colors, type specs, and chart parts for building charts at the North Carolina Department of Public Instruction — the quick reference, all on one page. Everything’s right below.
Just deciding which chart to use? Open the Chart Chooser

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
Anatomy of an NCDPI chart
Every part has a job. Title carries the takeaway; the subtitle carries the contract; color does one thing at a time.



Anatomy of an NCDPI chart — labeled callouts for the title, subtitle, axis labels, axis values, gridlines, data marks, annotation, and source note, each with its font size and color.


See Chart elements for the full anatomy, and Table and Dashboard elements for those.

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
Type at a glance
Arial throughout. Sizes and colors are the brand standard — the same values that drive every chart in this guide.

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
How we use color
Reach for the palette that matches the job, not your favorite color. Each panel below is the same idea: grey recedes, color carries the message.


Highlight — one navy #003A70 bar against neutral grey. Draw the eye to one thing. → Highlights
Categorical — distinct, unordered groups (regions, subjects). → Category groups
Sequence — an ordered low-to-high scale (counts, bands). → Sequences
Good / Bad — a value worth celebrating (teal #077890) or flagging (rust #B33A12). → Sequences · Diverging


Five distinct colors is the ceiling — three or four is better. Past five hues people can’t reliably tell categories apart. Don’t add colors; highlight one against grey, or use small multiples.


Category groups
Brand order — Blue → Orange → Purple → Green → Gold → Plum:



Highlight & chart elements


Sequences and diverging ramps come in 2–5 and 3–9 steps — pick a length on the Sequences and Diverging pages. Every value is in the searchable Color lookup.

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
Take the specs with you
The full spec sheet — every palette, hex, font, and size — as a single file you can keep, print, or feed to an AI assistant. Generated from the same source as this page, so it never drifts.
⬇ Download (Markdown) ⬇ Download (JSON)
Tableau users: load our palettes straight into Tableau. Download the file below, then copy its contents into your Preferences.tps (in Documents/My Tableau Repository/) and restart Tableau — our categorical, sequential, and diverging palettes will be waiting in the color menu.
⬇ Download Tableau palettes (.tps)

title: The NCDPI chart cheat sheet
text:
Go deeper

Chart Chooser — find the right chart for your data Chart types — worked examples by purpose Color — the four palette jobs in depth Chart parts — anatomy of charts, tables, dashboards Best practices — the construction rules and the evidence Gallery — every chart in the guide, on one page
